_comment: SYNTHETIC fixture (hand-written, no real student data, no PII). A good Foxy 'explain' exchange: grounded in NCERT Class 8 Science, step-by-step, anticipates a misconception, gives the mechanism, cites the chapter, EN in / EN out, AI identity maintained. Expected: FX-O2 deterministic pass; LLM-judged criteria pass on a full-judge run. Calibration note (REG-256): NCERT Class 8 Science treats the speed of sound QUALITATIVELY, so this fixture uses the qualitative 'light travels much faster than sound' framing — no 340 m/s / 300,000 km/s / 3-seconds-per-km numerics (those belong to Class 9 Sound). The NCERT citation is deliberately chapter-number-FREE: the repo's syllabus manifest (supabase/migrations/20260624000100_seed_cbse_syllabus_manifest.sql) pins Sound as Class 8 Science Ch. 13 in the PRE-rationalized 18-chapter list, which disagrees with the rationalized edition's numbering, so no chapter number is asserted.
chat_response:
Great question! Let's work through why we see lightning before we hear thunder, step by step.

Step 1 — Two signals, one event. A lightning strike produces light and sound at the same moment.

Step 2 — Light travels much faster than sound. Light is far, far quicker than sound, so the flash reaches your eyes almost instantly, while the sound of thunder takes noticeably longer to travel through the air to your ears. This speed difference is the mechanism: both signals leave together, but light finishes the journey first and sound lags behind.

Step 3 — The gap hints at distance. The farther away the storm is, the longer the thunder takes to reach you — so a long gap between the flash and the rumble means a distant storm, and a shrinking gap means the storm is coming closer.

Careful here — a common mix-up: it is NOT that thunder happens after lightning. They happen together; we just receive the light first.

You can read more in the Sound chapter of NCERT Class 8 Science — especially the part about how sound travels through air and other materials.

Quick check for you: if the storm is moving closer, will the gap between the flash and the thunder get longer or shorter? I'm an AI study buddy, so if you want to explore how sound travels through water or steel, just ask — or check with your teacher for a class demonstration.

artifact.subject: science
artifact.student_question: Why do we see lightning first and hear thunder later?

Step 2 — Light travels much faster than sound: the flash reaches your eyes almost instantly, while the sound of thunder takes noticeably longer to travel through the air to your ears; both signals leave together, but light finishes the journey first and sound lags behind. Step 3 — The gap hints at distance: the farther away the storm is, the longer the thunder takes to reach you, so a long gap means a distant storm and a shrinking gap means it is coming closer. Careful here — a common mix-up: it is NOT that thunder happens after lightning; they happen together, we just receive the light first.
